findings.summary: OCR worker._update_paperless_document manually manages pipeline tags instead of using common.tags.clean_pipeline_tags like the classifier worker does
evidence: ocr/worker.py lines 197-200 manually discards PRE_TAG_ID and OCR_PROCESSING_TAG_ID and adds POST_TAG_ID, while classifier/worker.py line 278 delegates to clean_pipeline_tags(). If a new pipeline tag is added to Settings, ocr/worker.py will silently fail to strip it.
findings.suggestion: Replace the manual tag manipulation in _update_paperless_document with clean_pipeline_tags() followed by adding POST_TAG_ID, matching the classifier worker pattern.

findings.summary: preflight.py imports the private _openai_holder from llm.py, bypassing the module's public API
evidence: preflight.py line 10: 'from .llm import _openai_holder' — the leading underscore signals internal use, yet preflight reaches in directly. The module exposes init_openai_client() publicly but has no corresponding public get accessor.
findings.suggestion: Add a public get_openai_client() function in llm.py and have preflight.py use that instead of importing the private holder.

findings.summary: classifier/constants.py re-exports REFUSAL_PHRASES as ERROR_PHRASES creating a redundant alias
evidence: classifier/constants.py line 48: 'ERROR_PHRASES: tuple[str, ...] = REFUSAL_PHRASES' — this is a simple alias with no transformation. quality_gates.py then imports ERROR_PHRASES. The rename adds cognitive overhead without semantic benefit since both names refer to the same refusal phrases.
findings.suggestion: Have quality_gates.py import REFUSAL_PHRASES directly from common.constants and remove the ERROR_PHRASES alias from classifier/constants.py.

findings.summary: The openai SDK is a heavy transitive dependency (pulls in httpx, pydantic, etc.) used even in Ollama-only deployments where a lighter HTTP client would suffice
evidence: library_setup.py creates an openai.OpenAI client with api_key='dummy' and base_url pointing to Ollama. The full openai SDK (with pydantic models, type stubs, etc.) is loaded even though Ollama only needs a simple chat completions POST. httpx is already a direct dependency.
findings.suggestion: Acceptable trade-off for API compatibility, but worth documenting in pyproject.toml that openai is required even for Ollama deployments.

findings.summary: Stats reset responsibility is inconsistently placed: OCR worker calls reset_stats() after claiming the lock, classifier worker calls it before refreshing the document
evidence: classifier/worker.py line 84: reset_stats() is called at the top of process() before get_document(). ocr/worker.py line 80: reset_stats() is called after claim_processing_tag succeeds. This means an OCR early-exit (error tag check) leaves stale stats, while classifier always starts clean.
findings.suggestion: Move reset_stats() to a consistent position in both workers — either as the first action in process() (matching classifier) or immediately before the first provider call.

findings.summary: OCR and classifier daemons use different patterns for per-document processing closures, making the shared daemon_loop harder to reason about
evidence: ocr/daemon.py defines _process_document as a module-level function and wraps it in a lambda (line 67). classifier/daemon.py defines process_document as a closure inside main() capturing taxonomy_cache. The module-level vs closure asymmetry is a minor coherence gap.
findings.suggestion: Adopt a consistent pattern — either both use closures capturing shared state or both use module-level functions with explicit parameters.

findings.summary: Both worker classes extract doc_id and title from the doc dict in __init__ but also store the full doc dict, creating partially-redundant state
evidence: classifier/worker.py lines 58-64 stores self.doc, self.doc_id = doc['id'], self.title = doc.get('title'). ocr/worker.py lines 58-64 does the same, plus later reassigns self.doc = document (line 63 of process). The doc_id/title caching is fine for logging but the pattern of re-fetching and overwriting self.doc mid-flight (OCR worker line 63) is surprising.
findings.suggestion: In the OCR worker, stop overwriting self.doc after re-fetch, or rename the local to 'document' consistently and drop self.doc storage.

findings.summary: _update_paperless_document manually discards pre/processing tags and adds post tag, duplicating logic that clean_pipeline_tags + add already handles in the classifier worker
evidence: Lines 197-200 in ocr/worker.py: current_tags.discard(self.settings.PRE_TAG_ID); if self.settings.OCR_PROCESSING_TAG_ID: current_tags.discard(...); current_tags.add(self.settings.POST_TAG_ID) — ad-hoc tag surgery instead of using clean_pipeline_tags like the classifier worker does
findings.suggestion: Use clean_pipeline_tags(current_tags, self.settings) then add POST_TAG_ID, matching the pattern in classifier/worker.py _apply_classification. This centralizes the tag-transition logic.

findings.summary: _has_ocr_errors checks only REFUSAL_MARK (a single hardcoded string) instead of the full OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS list used during OCR
evidence: Line 173: is_error_content(text, (self.settings.REFUSAL_MARK,)) passes only the single REFUSAL_MARK string, while the OCR provider at line 107 checks against self.settings.OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS which includes all REFUSAL_PHRASES plus REFUSAL_MARK
findings.suggestion: Pass self.settings.OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS to is_error_content so the post-assembly error check uses the same phrase set as the per-page refusal detection.

findings.summary: retry decorator wrapper function has implicit None return on exhausted loop, relying on caller never reaching that path
evidence: Lines 32-56: the for-loop in wrapper() has no explicit return after the loop body; if MAX_RETRIES iterations complete without raising (impossible given the re-raise on last attempt), the function returns None silently. The type signature claims Callable[..., T] but mypy cannot verify the implicit unreachable path.
findings.suggestion: Add an explicit unreachable assertion after the for-loop (e.g. raise AssertionError('unreachable')) to satisfy type checkers and make the contract explicit.

findings.summary: Typo 'AssertionError' instead of 'AssertionError' in _create_named_item unreachable branch
evidence: Line 117: raise AssertionError(...) — this is not a valid Python builtin; it should be AssertionError. However, on closer inspection this may actually work as AssertionError IS the correct spelling — let me re-check. Actually 'AssertionError' is a TYPO — the correct name is 'AssertionError'. Wait — the correct Python exception is 'AssertionError'. Re-reading: the code says 'AssertionError' which IS the correct spelling.
findings.suggestion: Disregard — AssertionError is the correct spelling. No action needed.

findings.summary: update_document_metadata accepts **fields: Any but silently drops unknown keys, masking caller typos
evidence: Lines 168-182: the method accepts arbitrary **fields and filters through _METADATA_FIELDS, silently discarding any key not in the mapping. A caller passing 'correspondant_id' (typo) would get no error and no update. The DocumentMetadataUpdate TypedDict exists but is not enforced at the call site.
findings.suggestion: Add a warning log or raise TypeError for unexpected keys in **fields that are not in _METADATA_FIELDS, or use Unpack[DocumentMetadataUpdate] (Python 3.12+) to get static checking.

findings.summary: truncate_content_by_pages first segment always starts at 0, capturing preamble text before first page header, which may be duplicated if not expected
evidence: Lines 148-152: 'start = 0 if idx == 0 else match.start()' means the first segment includes everything before the first --- Page N --- header. For idx=1+, start is the match position. If there is content before the first page header, it is included in segment 0 but the preamble-before-first-header is an implicit assumption not visible from the API.
findings.suggestion: This is intentional design (captures preamble), but the comment on line 148 could note that preamble inclusion is deliberate. Minor — no functional defect.

findings.summary: Both daemons create a new PaperlessClient (and underlying httpx.Client) per document, adding connection overhead that a shared or pooled client would avoid
evidence: ocr/daemon.py line 20: PaperlessClient(settings) created fresh per document in _process_document
classifier/daemon.py line 67: PaperlessClient(settings) created fresh per document in process_document closure
findings.suggestion: Consider using a thread-local or per-worker PaperlessClient rather than per-document creation. The httpx.Client supports concurrent usage from multiple threads, but if isolation is needed, a ThreadLocal wrapper would reduce connection churn.

findings.summary: classifier/constants.py re-exports REFUSAL_PHRASES as ERROR_PHRASES, adding an unnecessary alias layer
evidence: ERROR_PHRASES: tuple[str, ...] = REFUSAL_PHRASES on line 48 of classifier/constants.py, then quality_gates.py imports ERROR_PHRASES instead of using REFUSAL_PHRASES directly
findings.suggestion: Import REFUSAL_PHRASES directly in quality_gates.py and remove the ERROR_PHRASES alias from classifier/constants.py

findings.summary: init_openai_client() is a trivial passthrough that just calls _openai_holder.init()
evidence: Line 44-45 of llm.py: def init_openai_client(client): _openai_holder.init(client) — no added policy, validation, or translation
findings.suggestion: Either import _openai_holder directly in library_setup.py (it is already accessed directly in other modules like preflight.py), or add the function to the holder class as a classmethod to justify the indirection

findings.summary: OCR worker class is named DocumentProcessor while classifier worker class is ClassificationProcessor — sibling modules use different naming conventions
evidence: ocr/worker.py defines DocumentProcessor; classifier/worker.py defines ClassificationProcessor; both serve identical roles as per-document orchestrators
findings.suggestion: Rename OCR's DocumentProcessor to OcrProcessor (or rename ClassificationProcessor to DocumentProcessor in classifier) for consistent naming across sibling modules

findings.summary: OCR stats reset is called inside DocumentProcessor.process() (worker level) while classifier stats reset is also in ClassificationProcessor.process(), but OCR creates the provider per-document in daemon.py making the reset redundant
evidence: ocr/daemon.py line 21: OcrProvider is created fresh per-document call, so reset_stats() on line 80 of worker.py resets a brand-new instance; classifier/daemon.py line 68 also creates ClassificationProvider per-document, same pattern — both resets are no-ops on fresh instances
findings.suggestion: Remove the reset_stats() calls from both worker process() methods since providers are created fresh per-document, or move providers to be shared across the batch and reset at batch boundaries

findings.summary: daemon_loop.py catches a specific list of exceptions (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, RuntimeError, ValueError, TypeError) but misses KeyError and other plausible failures from JSON parsing and dict access
evidence: Line 106: except (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, RuntimeError, ValueError, TypeError) — fetch_work() calls iter_documents_by_pipeline_tag which does doc.get('id') and client.get_documents_by_tag which calls response.json(), but KeyError from malformed API responses would crash the loop
findings.suggestion: Either use a broader base exception (Exception) with a re-raise for KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit, or add KeyError to the tuple

findings.summary: Error handling catch clauses use inconsistent exception tuples across modules for the same kind of operation (Paperless API calls)
evidence: tags.py get_latest_tags catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError, ValueError); preflight.py _check_paperless_reachable catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError); claims.py catches (OSError, httpx.HTTPError) throughout — ValueError is included inconsistently for the same type of Paperless API call
findings.suggestion: Define a single RETRYABLE_PAPERLESS_EXCEPTIONS tuple (like RETRYABLE_HTTP_EXCEPTIONS in paperless.py) and use it consistently in all catch clauses wrapping Paperless API calls

findings.summary: TaxonomyCache._get_or_create_item_id and get_or_create_tag_ids catch bare Exception instead of specific types
evidence: Line 192: except Exception — catches everything including programming errors; line 235: except Exception — same pattern; both are around API create calls where httpx.HTTPError or httpx.HTTPStatusError would be the expected failure
findings.suggestion: Narrow to (httpx.HTTPError, OSError) to match the error handling convention used elsewhere for Paperless API calls

findings.summary: paperless.py line 117 raises AssertionError (misspelled) instead of AssertionError — this is actually a NameError at runtime
evidence: Line 117: raise AssertionError(...) — should be AssertionError is not a built-in, the correct name is AssertionError. Wait — actually checking: Python builtin is AssertionError. Let me re-read: 'raise AssertionError' — this is misspelled, the correct builtin is 'AssertionError'. Actually the Python builtin IS 'AssertionError'. Rechecking the actual text: 'AssertionError' vs 'AssertionError' — the code says 'AssertionError' which IS the correct Python builtin name.
findings.suggestion: Disregard — on re-examination, AssertionError is indeed spelled correctly in the source as 'AssertionError' which matches Python's builtin. No fix needed.
